bins.name: Reward distributed flexibility in interconnection
bins.desc: The filers urge FERC to direct RTOs/ISOs to expedite studies for large loads that bring curtailable or dispatchable distributed assets, and to broaden Principle Seven so it counts incremental distributed capacity such as load flexibility, storage, and generation. They want all available resources, not only traditional generation, recognized in the reform.
quotes: the Distributed Capacity Parties respectfully urge the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC” or “Commission”) in any subsequent rulemaking2 to direct the Regional Transmission Organizations/Independent System Operators (“RTO/ISOs”) to develop mechanisms for granting expedited interconnection studies to large loads that provide curtailable and/or dispatchable distributed assets

The ANOPR Seventh Principle4 should include incremental Distributed Capacity in the form of load flexibility, storage, and/or generation capacity whose location can address capacity and transmission constraints projected to be caused by the new large loads, even if such capacity is located on the distribution system.
ANOPR Principle Seven should be broadened to leverage all available resources.

bins.name: Count electrically proximate capacity
bins.desc: The filers argue supporting capacity need not be physically co-located but can be distributed across the area where the load affects constraints, so long as the operator can control it. They ask that electrically proximate or adjacent assets count in study prioritization and incentives, and that such capacity be geographically transferable via wholesale capacity credits.
quotes: The ANOPR Seventh Principle4 should include incremental Distributed Capacity in the form of load flexibility, storage, and/or generation capacity whose location can address capacity and transmission constraints projected to be caused by the new large loads, even if such capacity is located on the distribution system.

These comments endorse the above principles while expanding on them to ensure that “electrically proximate” or “adjacent” grid assets—assets that can mitigate the constraints imposed by new loads where those constraints actually occur—are also considered in study prioritization, netted against load withdrawals for study purposes, and included in speed-to-power incentive structures.

new capacity supporting large loads need not be physically co-located at the facility, but can be distributed across the geographic area within which the new load will impact system constraints, provided that the system operator has the requisite level of control to integrate the facility into operations and system planning.

capacity—including batteries, generation, and demand response (DR)—should be geographically transferable in the form of capacity credits as defined by the wholesale market, or within the TOs system planning paradigm.

bins.name: Extend co-location beyond the fence line
bins.desc: The filers treat co-location as a concept that should extend beyond the facility fence line, so capacity distributed across the relevant geographic area qualifies for the same treatment as physically co-located resources.
quotes: new capacity supporting large loads need not be physically co-located at the facility, but can be distributed across the geographic area within which the new load will impact system constraints, provided that the system operator has the requisite level of control to integrate the facility into operations and system planning.

bins.name: Broaden joint study to distributed resources
bins.desc: The filers endorse holistic study of load and generation but ask that the study scope explicitly include incremental distributed resources in the relevant grid area, and that those resources be netted against load withdrawals and considered in study prioritization.
quotes: These comments endorse the above principles while expanding on them to ensure that “electrically proximate” or “adjacent” grid assets—assets that can mitigate the constraints imposed by new loads where those constraints actually occur—are also considered in study prioritization, netted against load withdrawals for study purposes, and included in speed-to-power incentive structures.

the existing ANOPR Principle Three, “load and hybrid facilities should be studied together with generating facilities,” should explicitly include study of incremental resources in the relevant grid area,16 whether or not those resources look like traditional utility-scale power plants.

bins.name: Expedite studies for distributed-capacity loads
bins.desc: The filers ask FERC to direct RTOs/ISOs to create mechanisms granting expedited interconnection studies to large loads that provide curtailable or dispatchable distributed assets.
quotes: the Distributed Capacity Parties respectfully urge the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC” or “Commission”) in any subsequent rulemaking2 to direct the Regional Transmission Organizations/Independent System Operators (“RTO/ISOs”) to develop mechanisms for granting expedited interconnection studies to large loads that provide curtailable and/or dispatchable distributed assets

bins.name: Include distributed resources in joint study
bins.desc: The filers support the joint study of load and generation under Principle Three and ask that it explicitly extend to incremental distributed resources in the relevant grid area, regardless of whether those resources resemble utility-scale plants.
quotes: the existing ANOPR Principle Three, “load and hybrid facilities should be studied together with generating facilities,” should explicitly include study of incremental resources in the relevant grid area,16 whether or not those resources look like traditional utility-scale power plants.

bins.name: No position on FERC jurisdiction
bins.desc: The filers expressly decline to take a position on whether FERC has jurisdiction to issue a rulemaking on large load interconnection.
quotes: The Distributed Capacity Parties do not take a position on FERC’s jurisdiction.

bins.name: Distributed capacity deploys faster
bins.desc: The filers argue distributed capacity can be installed and aggregated into utility-scale assets in months rather than the years new generation requires, making it a bridge resource matched to the urgency of large-load growth.
quotes: Distributed capacity assets are installed today across critical footprints, are readily installable en masse, and may be aggregated into utility-scale assets on a timeline of months rather than years.

bins.name: Geographically transferable capacity credits
bins.desc: The filers propose that supporting capacity, including batteries, generation, and demand response, be made geographically transferable through wholesale capacity credits or within transmission owner system planning.
quotes: capacity—including batteries, generation, and demand response (DR)—should be geographically transferable in the form of capacity credits as defined by the wholesale market, or within the TOs system planning paradigm.

bins.name: Remove barriers to aggregated DER
bins.desc: The filers ask FERC to rectify barriers to aggregated resource participation in capacity, energy, and ancillary service markets, citing delays in Order No. 2222 implementation and utility data access issues. They note FERC could direct RTOs/ISOs to consider distributed capacity while leaving specifics to contracts with distribution utilities.
quotes: To unlock the capabilities of existing distributed capacity, barriers to participation by aggregated resources in capacity, energy, and ancillary service markets should be rectified. Delays in Order No. 2222 implementation, utility data access issues, and other challenges significantly limit the development of latent flexibility today.

FERC could direct the RTOs/ISOs to consider distributed capacity in connecting large loads, while specifics would likely involve contractual arrangements between a distribution utility, the large load, and the distributed capacity provider.
